topic: My online library account has fourteen books on hold at a branch in Vermont I have never visited and the holds have been renewing themselves automatically for seven years
title: My Library Account Has Fourteen Books on Hold in a Town I Have Never Visited. They Have Been Renewing for Seven Years. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I logged into my online library account on a Sunday afternoon to renew a cookbook and the dashboard showed fourteen other books on hold under my name at a branch in Putney, Vermont. I live in Indiana. I have never been to Vermont. The earliest hold dated to two thousand nineteen. Every one of them had been renewing itself quarterly for seven years. None of the books had ever been checked out and returned. They had only ever been held.
description:
I logged into my online library account on a Sunday afternoon to renew a cookbook and the dashboard showed fourteen other books on hold under my name at a branch in Putney, Vermont. I live in Indiana. I have never been to Vermont. The earliest hold dated to two thousand nineteen. Every one of them had been renewing itself quarterly for seven years. None of the books had ever been checked out and returned. They had only ever been held.

The fourteen titles overlapped my life in ways no consortium glitch could explain. A memoir of an only child whose mother had a stroke the same year mine did. A guide to mitigating radon in a nineteen fifty six bungalow. A restoration manual for the exact car I owned in graduate school. I called the Putney branch. The librarian confirmed an active patron in my name who picked up the holds every other Saturday at three in the afternoon and paid his fines in cash.

She emailed me a screenshot from the circulation security camera. The man at the desk had my face. The book he had just checked out was bound in plain cloth. The title on the spine was the exact title of this video. Watch to the end. There is a new card now at a branch in Belfast, Maine. The author on the most recent hold has the same face as the man in the photograph.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I logged into my online library account on a Sunday afternoon to renew a cookbook I had borrowed from my local branch. The dashboard loaded slowly on the laptop. When the page finished, the cookbook was at the top of the screen. Below the cookbook was a section I had never seen before. It was labeled holds at other branches. The number beside it was fourteen. I tapped to expand it. Fourteen titles. All of them on active hold under my name and my card number. The branch listed for every single one of them was Putney Public Library in Putney, Vermont. I live in Indiana. I have lived in Indiana my entire adult life. I have never been to Vermont. I have never owned a card at a library outside of my own county.

sections.narration: I live in a small city in central Indiana on a street of brick ranches built in the nineteen fifties. The local library is six blocks from my house. It is a low stone building from the same decade as the houses, with a circulation desk inside the front doors and a children's wing tucked behind a glass partition. I have held a card at that branch since I started college in two thousand seven. The card is a thin blue rectangle worn soft at the edges from nineteen years in my wallet. I usually borrow two or three books a month. Cookbooks in the spring. Mystery novels in the fall. I have never lost the card. I have never requested a replacement. I have never used it at any branch except my own.

sections.narration: My routine with the library account is the same every month. I open the app on my phone or the website on my laptop. I renew whatever is on my checked out shelf. I look at the recommended titles. I log out. The whole thing takes about three minutes from the couch in my living room. I have done this on the same couch for six years. The dashboard has never had unexpected entries on it. No mystery fines. No unfamiliar checkouts. No holds I did not place. The library system sends me an email when a hold is ready or a book is overdue. My inbox has a folder for those emails going back to two thousand fifteen. I had never received a single email from any branch other than my own.

sections.narration: I clicked into the holds list and read the entries one by one. Fourteen books. All of them at Putney Public Library, three Lebanon Street, Putney, Vermont. The first hold had been placed in March of two thousand nineteen. The second in June of the same year. The dates marched forward in a quiet quarterly rhythm. Every single hold was marked active. Every single one had been automatically renewed exactly twenty eight times. None of them had ever been picked up. None of them had ever been canceled. The system showed a column for the patron who had placed the hold. The column listed my full legal name. The card number was my own. The pickup location said branch counter, three p m, every other Saturday. I had never placed any of those holds.

sections.narration: My first thought was that a duplicate card number had been issued in another state. Library consortiums share records sometimes. Two systems can collide. A patron in Vermont could have been issued a card with the same digits as mine and the consortium database could have stitched our accounts together by accident. My second thought was that this was a simple display error. A bug in the dashboard pulling another patron's holds into my view. My third thought was that the cookbook renewal had triggered some kind of cross reference. I closed the browser. I opened it again. The fourteen holds were still there. I logged out completely. I logged back in. The fourteen holds were still there. I refreshed the page four times. The Putney branch was still listed. The patron name was still mine.

sections.narration: I read the fourteen titles slowly. None of them were books I had ever heard of. None of them were on any reading list of mine. But the topics overlapped my life with uncomfortable precision. The third hold was a memoir written by a woman who was an only child whose mother had a stroke in two thousand eighteen. My mother had a stroke in two thousand eighteen. The fifth hold was a homeowner guide to radon mitigation in nineteen fifty six era brick bungalows. My house is a nineteen fifty six brick bungalow. The seventh hold was a restoration manual for the nineteen eighty eight Honda Civic CRX. I owned a nineteen eighty eight Honda Civic CRX in graduate school. The eighth hold was about caring for a black short haired cat with kidney disease. My cat had kidney disease.

sections.narration: I found the Putney branch phone number on the consortium directory and I called it from the kitchen on Monday morning. A woman answered. I told her my name and my card number. I asked her to look up my account at her branch. There was a long pause while she typed. Then she said yes. The account had been active at Putney since the spring of two thousand eighteen. The address on file was a post office box in the village. The phone number on file was a Vermont area code I did not recognize. The patron picked up the fourteen holds in person every other Saturday at three in the afternoon. He paid his fines in cash at the desk. The last fine had been paid eleven days earlier. The account had never been delinquent.

sections.narration: I asked the librarian if she remembered the man personally. She said yes. She had been at the branch for fifteen years. She saw him every other Saturday at three. He came in alone. He was about my height. He wore the same gray canvas jacket year round. He paid his fines in cash, in folded bills. She remembered the last one because she had counted it twice. He had paid with a five dollar bill folded in thirds, the long way, with the portrait facing outward. That is the way I fold cash in my wallet. I fold every bill in my wallet that way and I have done it since college. She told me he always said thank you when he took the books. He never asked for a receipt. He never asked for a bag.

sections.narration: I asked the librarian if the branch had security cameras at the desk. She said the consortium had installed one above the circulation counter three years earlier. I asked if she could send me a still image from the most recent Saturday. She said she could. Ninety minutes later an email came through with a single jpeg attached. I opened it on the laptop in the kitchen. The image showed a man standing at the circulation counter from a slightly elevated angle. The gray canvas jacket. The hands resting on the counter folded the way mine fold. The face turned three quarters toward the camera. The face was my face. The book on the counter under his hand was thick and bound in plain dark cloth. The title printed on the spine was the exact title of this video I am narrating now.

sections.narration: I asked the librarian to close the Vermont card. She closed it that afternoon. She sent me a confirmation email with the deactivation timestamp. I did not sleep well for three weeks. On the morning of the twenty second day I logged into my consortium dashboard from the couch. There was a new card listed under my name at a branch in Belfast, Maine. Fourteen holds. All active. All set to renew quarterly. The titles had advanced. The third hold was a new memoir with my mother's exact stroke date in the subtitle. The fifth hold was about radon mitigation in my exact house. At the top of the list was a memoir I had not seen before. The author photograph on the inside flap matched the photograph the Putney librarian sent me. The book is titled with the exact phrase I am saying at the end of this sentence.
